Judge: John M. True; Clerk: V. da Fonseca; Not reported. (301/JMT)

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Plaintiff Dillon Johnstone'S Responses To Requests For Production Of Documents, Set One
Off Calendar Jun-11-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Atty. BOONE CALLAWAY, Esq. of Callaway & Wolf (tel: 415-541-0300) for the Plaintiff.
Atty. MARK SANDBERG, Esq. of Bowman and Brooke LLP (tel: 408-279-5393) for Defendants Uber Technologies, Inc; Rasier, LLC and Rasier-CA, LLC.
Atty. JOYCE CLIFFORD, Esq. of Bremer Whyte Brown & O'Meara LLP (tel: 510-540-4881) for Defendant Tushar Tambe.

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT TUSHAR TAMBE's Motion To Compel Plaintiff Dillon Johnstone's Responses To Requests For Production Of Documents, Set One.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant Tushar Tambe's unopposed motion to compel further responses to his requests for production of documents, set one, from plaintiff Dillon Johnstone and for sanctions is granted in part. Defendant shows good cause for production. Johnstone is ordered to provide further responses to RFP Nos. 1, 3, 4, 5, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12, 13, 18, 19, 20, 30, 32. All responsive documents shall be produced within 20 days of entry of this order.

Some of Johnstone's RFP responses suggest he no longer seeks recovery for lost earnings. If that is the case, he must make it clear.

For the above-listed RFPs plus RFP No. 2, Johnstone shall produce all responsive documents within 20 days of entry of this order.

For the following RFPs, Johnstone shall amend his response to confirm he has conducted a reasonably diligent search: 21, 22, 23, 24, 25, 26, 27, 29, 31.

For the following responses, Johnstone's objections have merit or his responses are complete; no further response is ordered: 16, 17.

Tambe seeks sanctions. CCP 2031.300(c) provides that "the court shall impose a monetary sanction . . . against any party, person, or attorney who unsuccessfully makes or opposes a motion to compel a response to a demand for inspection . . . unless it finds that the one subject to the sanction acted with substantial justification or that other circumstances make the imposition of the sanction unjust." Sanctions may be awarded even where the nonmoving party does not oppose the motion. (Rule of Court 3.1348(a).) Tambe's sanctions request is reasonable and the court does not find substantial justification. The court orders Johnstone to pay $1290 to Tambe within 20 days of entry of this order.

Order submitted.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (301/CVA)

Notice Of Motion For Protective Order
Off Calendar Mar-12-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances via Zoom Conference
R. Boone Callaway, Esq., CALLAWAY & WOLF (415) 541-0300 boone@callawayandwolf.com Attorney for Plaintiff Dillon Johnstone
Mark S. Sandberg, Esq BOWMAN AND BROOKE LLP (408) 279-5393 Mark.Sandberg@bowmanandbrooke.com
Attorney for Defendants, UBER TECHNOLOGIES, INC., RASIER, LLC; and RASIER-CA, LLC
Bryan Stofferahn, Esq., BREMER WHYTE BROWN & O'MEARA LLP (510) 540-4881 bstofferahn@bremerwhyte.com Attorneys for Defendant, TUSHAR PRABHAKAR TAMBE
Sheila Pham, CSR# Court reporter, CSR # 1329, 415-274-9977 calendar-la@veritex.com, Court Reporter

Defendant Uber contests the Court's tentative ruling.

Hearing is held. Argued. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument modifies and adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF DILLON JOHNSTONE'S Motion For Protective Order

Plaintiff Dillon Johnstone seeks a protective order limiting the discovery that Uber Technologies, Inc.; Rasier LLC; and Rasier-CA, LLC may serve on him and relieving him of the obligation to respond to already-propounded discovery by Uber. Those three defendants oppose and seek sanctions. The court grants Johnstone's motion in part and denies it in part.

Uber has propounded 228 special interrogatories, 138 requests for admission, and 145 requests for production of documents. This is a plainly excessive number in light of the claims and defenses in this case. The court finds Johnstone's meet and confer efforts sufficient in light of the parties' respective positions in those letters and in the motion papers; no continued efforts at informal resolution would have bridged this gap. The court grants the protective order in part, limiting Uber to no more than 40 special interrogatories, 40 requests for admission, and 55 requests for production absent further order of the court. The topics Uber has covered with its current discovery can easily be combined into this or a smaller number of requests that will yield no less information but be considerably less burdensome to answer. Johnstone is relieved of responding to the discovery attached at Exhibits A through C to the Calloway declaration. Unless Uber elects by 5 p.m. on March 13, 2025 to go forward with the already propounded discovery and specifies which 40 SR's, 40 RFA's and 40 RFP's Plaintiff shall respond to. If Uber so elects, Plaintiff's responses are due April 7, 2025.

Johnstone asks the court to limit all defendants to the number it has set. The court does not grant relief against parties who have not yet propounded discovery but urges the parties to apply the guidance in this order to future discovery disputes.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Christine Van Aken, Clerk: W. Trupek, Court Reporter: Sheila Pham, CSR# Court reporter, CSR # 1329, 415-274-9977 calendar-la@veritex.com, Reported. (302/RBU)
